title: Understanding Clay Shrinkage and How to Measure It

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage by which clay reduces in size as it loses moisture during drying, typically 8 to 15 percent for air-dry clays.
A test strip cut from fresh clay and measured before and after drying to calculate how much a particular batch of clay shrinks.
Clay that has dried to a firm but not fully hard state, stiff enough to hold its shape while still slightly damp enough to carve cleanly.

content.text: Air-dry clay shrinks as it dries. Knowing your clay's
shrinkage rate

is not optional when you are making a lid for a box, a set of matching pieces, or anything that must fit alongside something else. Without a measurement you are guessing, and the guess is usually wrong. A
shrinkage bar
takes five minutes to make and gives you a reliable number for the entire batch.

materials.name: Air-dry clay
materials.quantity: small offcut from the working batch

content.text: Preparation

Take a small piece of clay from the same batch you are using for your project. Roll or pat it into a flat strip roughly 10 mm thick. You will cut a precise rectangle from this strip in step one. The clay must be fresh and soft, not already at the
leather-hard
stage, because you need to record its wet dimensions before any drying has begun.

Use the fettling knife and ruler to cut a clean rectangle exactly 100 mm long and 20 mm wide. Precise edges matter because you will compare this measurement to the dried piece. Mark one end with a small notch using the needle tool so you can tell which end is which after drying.

Measure the length with the ruler while the clay is still wet and write it down. For a carefully cut bar this should read exactly 100 mm. Record it anyway, because cutting by hand is never perfectly precise.

Leave the bar to dry fully on a flat, non-porous surface such as a tile or a piece of glass. Allow at least 24-48 hours depending on room humidity. The bar is ready when it feels light, sounds hollow when tapped, and is the same temperature as the surrounding air.

Measure the dry length. If the bar started at 100 mm and now measures 88 mm, the shrinkage is 12 mm divided by 100 mm, which equals 12 percent. Write this on the bag or packet of clay so you have it for every project made from that batch.

To use the shrinkage rate when planning, divide your target finished size by the decimal equivalent of what remains after shrinkage. If shrinkage is 12 percent then 88 percent of the clay remains (0.88). For a lid intended to fit a 80 mm opening, cut the lid at 80 divided by 0.88, which is approximately 91 mm. This accounts for the clay pulling inward as it dries.

For a matching set, make all pieces from the same batch and at the same stage of freshness. Clay that has been stored longer can behave differently from a freshly opened pack, so run a new shrinkage bar whenever you open a new batch.
What success looks like

You have a number: a percentage you can apply to every piece made from this batch. Lids fit their boxes within 1-2 mm without forcing. Pieces made together as a set look proportionally consistent once dry.
Common mistakes
Testing a different batch than the one you are using: shrinkage rate varies between brands, formulations, and even between packs from the same brand. Always test the actual batch in use.
Measuring too soon: damp clay reads longer than dry clay, so the shrinkage figure comes out too small.
Applying the shrinkage correction in the wrong direction: always make the fresh piece larger than the finished size you want, not smaller.

items.problem: Lid is too small after drying
items.cause: Shrinkage not accounted for when cutting
items.fix: Calculate the required fresh size using the shrinkage bar result and re-cut the lid. A slight gap is easier to fix with a clay collar than a lid that is too small to close.

items.problem: Matching pieces are different sizes after drying
items.cause: Pieces cut at different times from clay at different moisture levels
items.fix: Cut all pieces for a set in the same session from the same fresh-opened batch, then dry them together under the same conditions.

items.cause: Bar dried too fast on one side
items.fix: Place the bar on a slightly porous surface such as a plaster bat or a cloth-covered board, and keep it away from direct airflow while it dries.
